summary: O'Neil argues that mathematicians must proactively define success metrics for calculus MOOCs before administrators impose purely financial measures. She draws a parallel to the failures of Value-Added Models in K-12 education, where poorly defined metrics have caused widespread harm. The post proposes randomized experiments comparing different teaching methods with pre-defined success criteria that go beyond test scores to include student experience and conceptual understanding. She acknowledges the irony that mathematicians studying MOOCs might accelerate their adoption, but argues it's better to shape the outcome than let others control it.
key_points: Large-scale educational models require fixed definitions of success, and if mathematicians don't define them, administrators will default to financial metrics
Value-Added Models in K-12 teaching serve as a cautionary tale of what happens when success metrics are poorly defined and opaque
Success metrics should be flexible enough to acknowledge different contexts need different outcomes
Experiments should be designed with pre-registered metrics, including oral exams and student attitudes, not just final exam scores
Taking control of the research now is preferable to having solutions imposed later
money_quotes: The problem with large-scale models is that they are easier to build if you have some fixed definition of success against which to optimize.
If we mathematicians don't get busy thinking this through, my fear is that administrations will do it for us, and will come up with things based strictly on money and not so much on pedagogy.
Let's keep things as complicated as they need to be to get things right!
One might complain that, if we do this, we're actually speeding on our own deaths by accelerating the MOOCs in the classroom. But I think it's important we take control before someone else does.
Design the questions you will ask your students and yourself before the semester begins so as to practice good model design – we don't want to decide on our metric after the fact.
Value-Added Models have no defined metric of success and are tearing up a generation of students and teachers.
key_insight: When large-scale educational systems require optimization metrics, the people who understand pedagogy must define success before administrators reduce it to cost savings.
